notes: Jacob Alexander Haller Jr. (1927-2018) received his MD from Johns Hopkins in 1951, returned to complete his surgical residency at Hopkins (1955-1959) after military service, then joined the University of Louisville faculty. In 1963, Blalock recruited Haller back to Hopkins to found the Division of Pediatric Surgery — one of the first accredited pediatric surgery training programs in the US. Haller served as inaugural Robert Garrett Professor of Pediatric Surgery and Surgeon-in-Charge of the Children's Medical and Surgical Center for nearly 30 years. He established the first US Children's Trauma Centre (1973) and contributed to the first PALS protocol. Conte (JTCVS 2012, PMID 22248679) lists Haller among Blalock's division chiefs. The Hopkins Medicine obituary (2018) and BAPS obituary confirm his training under Blalock. This edge connects the Blalock hub to the pediatric surgery subspecialty. TARGET MODULE: 02_general_surgery_spread.json

C. Rollins Hanlon trained under Blalock at Johns Hopkins and co-developed the Blalock-Hanlon operation (1948) for transposition of the great vessels. The ACS biographical profile confirms Hanlon later served as ACS Executive Director (1969-1986) and ACS President (1987-1988), making him one of the most powerful figures in American surgical governance during that period. Conte (JTCVS 2012, PMID 22248679) documents the Blalock training context. The ACS profile explicitly describes Hanlon working with Blalock on congenital heart operations at Hopkins. This edge connects the Blalock training hub to the ACS governance backbone (Hanlon had a direct governance role at the ACS already in the graph as a major node). TARGET MODULE: 02_general_surgery_spread.json

Hanlon served as Executive Director of the American College of Surgeons from 1969 to 1986, then as ACS President 1987-1988. The ACS archives profile on Blalock explicitly identifies Hanlon as 'ACS Director (1969-1986) and President (1987-1988).' As Executive Director for 17 years followed by the presidency, Hanlon was the most influential ACS administrator of the late 20th century. This edge creates a Blalock-Hanlon-ACS chain that connects the Hopkins training hub to the governance backbone through a different pathway than the existing Sabiston-ACS edge. TARGET MODULE: 12_governance_societies.json

James R. Jude was a surgical resident at Johns Hopkins under Blalock. In 1960, together with William Kouwenhoven and G. Guy Knickerbocker, Jude co-developed external cardiac massage (closed-chest CPR) at Hopkins — one of the most consequential medical innovations of the 20th century. Conte (JTCVS 2012, PMID 22248679) lists Jude among Blalock's division chiefs. The PBS 'Partners of the Heart' documentary identifies Jude in the Blalock trainee cohort. Confidence moderate due to approximated residency dates. The CPR innovation alone makes Jude one of the highest-impact trainees in the graph despite being a less prominent figure than Sabiston or Cooley in traditional surgical lineage narratives. TARGET MODULE: 02_general_surgery_spread.json

William H. Muller Jr. was a Blalock chief resident at Hopkins who subsequently became chairman of the Department of Surgery at the University of Virginia. Conte (JTCVS 2012, PMID 22248679) explicitly lists Muller among Blalock's department chairmen alongside Sabiston, Longmire, and Greenfield. Exact residency dates approximated. Confidence moderate due to estimated dates and limited published biographical detail beyond the Conte citation. TARGET MODULE: 02_general_surgery_spread.json
